text.title: Psalm Taint Analysis as Injection Backstop
text.summary: Psalm tracks user-controlled data from request inputs to dangerous sinks; CI fails on any unsuppressed taint.

section.title: Taint flow as a CI gate
section.p: Psalm's `--taint-analysis` mode marks `$_GET`, `$_POST`, request bodies, and user-supplied path parts as tainted, then propagates taint through method calls. A taint reaching `mysqli::query`, `exec`, `eval`, `unserialize`, or HTML output without `htmlspecialchars` raises a `TaintedSql`/`TaintedShell`/`TaintedHtml` issue.

rule.statement: CI MUST run `vendor/bin/psalm --taint-analysis --threads=4` on every PR; any taint issue blocks merge. Suppressing a taint (`@psalm-taint-escape`) is allowed ONLY when the call site applies a cited sanitizer, and the suppression line MUST include a comment naming the sanitizer (e.g. `// taint-escape: htmlspecialchars`).
rule.rationale: Type checkers cannot detect SQLi or XSS because the dangerous string is well-typed `string`; only data-flow analysis catches the pattern. Forcing a named-sanitizer comment on every escape makes the audit trail self-explanatory.

example.note: Parameterized query + htmlspecialchars; Psalm reports clean.
Tainted `$id` flows into raw SQL; Psalm raises `TaintedSql`.

antipattern.description: Wholesale `@psalm-suppress TaintedSql` annotations to silence the analyzer without a sanitizer.
antipattern.reason: Suppressions disable the gate; the bug remains, the audit trail is empty. Require named-sanitizer comments and reject suppressions that lack one.

section.reference: Psalm config used by this rule.
